metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the construction-time guard includes an explicit `self._model_override is None` (or equivalent structural exclusion) to exclude test-double paths, rather than relying on TestModel's implicit lack of a `.profile` attribute.
findings.finding: The model_override (test-double) exclusion from the construction-time capability check relies on an implicit mechanism not stated in the plan. The plan says the check is a NO-OP for `model_override` but the described guard `not isinstance(model, str)` is TRUE for any pydantic-ai TestModel (which is not a string), so the check body WOULD be entered for a test double. The only thing that would save it is TestModel lacking a `.profile` attribute (the `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` guard), but the plan presents the NO-OP as a structural property of the check, not as an implicit hasattr fallback. The plan needs to specify an explicit structural exclusion (e.g. `self._model_override is None`) as part of the guard condition, or acknowledge that `.profile` absence on TestModel is the intended exclusion mechanism.
findings.location: What section – Construction-time provider tool-calling check; AC line `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model`
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the guard description to: `self._model_override is None and not isinstance(model, str) and hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` — making the model_override exclusion structural and explicit, independent of whether the test double's type ever gains a `.profile`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `model.profile.supports_tools` exists on pydantic-ai 1.107.0's `AnthropicModel` by adding a minimal import probe in the test file (e.g. `from pydantic_ai.models.anthropic import AnthropicModel; assert hasattr(AnthropicModel(...).profile, 'supports_tools')`) or citing a pinned library source reference — the inline experiment log is not a codebase-verifiable artifact.
findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is asserted as a 'VERIFIED pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal' — the entire construction-time capability check is built on this attribute — but there are zero hits for `profile` or `supports_tools` anywhere in the rebar codebase (no import, no usage, no test). The only evidence is the plan's own inline experiment log, making this empirically unverifiable from the repo.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; AC: `test_capability_check_fails_fast`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line import+assertion probe in `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py` that instantiates an `AnthropicModel` stub (or reads `AnthropicModel.__init__` signature) and asserts `.profile` has `supports_tools`, so the test suite itself catches any future library rename. Also cite the specific pydantic-ai source file/line (e.g. `pydantic_ai/models/anthropic.py`) in the plan's verified-behavior log.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `model.profile.supports_tools` is a real, stable pydantic-ai `>=1,<2` attribute by citing its source path in `pydantic_ai` (e.g. `pydantic_ai/models/anthropic.py` or `pydantic_ai/profiles.py`) or a pip-inspectable import like `from pydantic_ai.profiles import ModelProfile`; add a `hasattr` guard in the implementation so a missing attribute degrades to a no-op rather than an `AttributeError` crash.
findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is asserted as a 'VERIFIED pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal' but has zero codebase usage and the only evidence is the plan's own inline experiment log — no external anchor (source-code path, GitHub link, or importable symbol) is cited. The pyproject.toml pins `pydantic-ai-slim>=1,<2` (not `==1.107.0`), so the attribute's existence must hold across the entire `>=1,<2` range for the construction-time check to be safe; a missing or renamed attribute on any in-range version would cause an `AttributeError` crash on the Anthropic path (not a graceful `LLMConfigError`).
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; AC: `test_capability_check_fails_fast` / `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model` / `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: In the implementation, wrap the attribute access defensively: `if hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools') and model.profile.supports_tools is False: raise LLMConfigError(...)` — this already matches the plan's stated guard condition (`hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')`), but make sure the plan's AC tests also verify the `AttributeError`-safe path (i.e. a model object with no `.profile` attribute is a no-op, not a crash). Additionally, cite a concrete import path for `model.profile` from the installed pydantic-ai package to nail the evidence class to Verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC + named test (e.g., `test_capability_check_noop_for_model_override`) that passes a mock/test-double object as `model` and asserts the capability check is skipped (no raise, no cache entry written) — covering the explicitly-promised NO-OP guarantee for the model_override path.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly states the capability check is a NO-OP for `model_override` (a test double), but no Acceptance Criterion or named test covers this path. If the guard condition (`not isinstance(model, str)` and `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')`) misbehaves on a test-double object that lacks `.profile` entirely (e.g., raises AttributeError rather than silently skipping), the check would crash in test runs — contradicting the stated NO-OP guarantee — without any test catching it.
findings.location: AC section — `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model` + plan body paragraph 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check' (model_override mention)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: `test_capability_check_noop_for_model_override` — construct a simple mock object (not a string, lacking `.profile.supports_tools`) and assert the check exits silently. Also consider tightening the guard to `isinstance(model, str) or not hasattr(model, 'profile') or not hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` to make the NO-OP behavior robust to attribute-absent test doubles.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Anchor the `model.profile.supports_tools` capability assertion to a verifiable external source (e.g. a pydantic-ai GitHub permalink to `ModelProfile` in the 1.107.0 tag, or the public API docs URL) and add a test that directly imports `AnthropicModel`, constructs one offline, and asserts `hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` — so a name-change is caught rather than silently disabling the guard.
findings.finding: The plan's central capability assertion — that `model.profile.supports_tools` is a reliable bool attribute on a pydantic-ai `AnthropicModel` object in version 1.107.0 — is cited only from a self-contained inline experiment log with no external anchor (no GitHub source URL, no API docs link, no changelog reference). There are zero hits for `supports_tools` or `model.profile` anywhere in the codebase. This is the sole mechanism behind the construction-time guard; a wrong attribute name silently makes the check a permanent NO-OP (the plan's own `hasattr` guard suppresses AttributeError).
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the test file: `from pydantic_ai.models.anthropic import AnthropicModel; m = AnthropicModel('claude-3-haiku-20240307', provider=...); assert hasattr(m, 'profile') and isinstance(m.profile.supports_tools, bool)` — this pins the attribute contract and fails loudly if pydantic-ai renames it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact 'non-empty result' predicate for each output mode (text/structured/findings) co-located with the usage-plausibility warning specification, so the guard condition is unambiguous across all three branches.
findings.finding: The 'non-empty result' predicate that gates the usage-plausibility WARNING is referenced as a decision rule but never defined. runner.py has three distinct output-mode branches (text → SimpleNamespace message list, structured → a validated model instance, findings → finalize_outcome dict), and 'non-empty' means something different for each. The plan provides no concrete predicate (e.g., 'output string is truthy', 'structured payload is not None', 'findings list len > 0') co-located with the warning logic, leaving the implementer to guess.
findings.location: What section — 'Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING'; AC line 'test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'non-empty result means: for mode=text, the output string is non-empty; for mode=structured, the returned object is not None; for mode=findings, the finalize_outcome result contains at least one key beyond _usage. The check fires AFTER finalize_outcome / _extract_usage returns.'

findings.finding: The plan's central unverified capability assertion — 'model.profile.supports_tools (a bool)' — is cited only from an inline experiment log with no external anchor, and the plan does not specify what Python object 'model' refers to at the check site in runner.py's flow. On the Anthropic path, the model object is constructed inside _build_retrying_anthropic_model (returns AnthropicModel); on other paths pydantic-ai resolves the model from a string internally. The cache is keyed by 'resolved model string', but the .profile check must be on a constructed model object — the mapping between string key and object is not specified, creating a real implementation ambiguity.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check' paragraph; Verified library behavior section

findings.finding: The `test_capability_check_cached` AC states the check is 'invoked once per model string, asserted by a spy' but never specifies what the spy intercepts or how caching is observable. The cache is a module-level `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` set — the only observable side-effect is whether the `model.profile.supports_tools` access (or the set.add) is called once vs twice. The plan names no intercept point (not the set operations, not the model construction, not a helper function) leaving the test mechanism structurally undefined.
findings.location: AC line `test_capability_check_cached`; What section — 'CACHE: a module-level set()'
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the check into `_check_tool_capability(resolved: str, model_obj) -> None` in `runner.py`; the test monkeypatches this function and asserts call count == 1 after two runner invocations with the same model string. The module-level set is cleared in the test fixture via `runner_mod._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.clear()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default cache-key derivation for model objects in the plan (e.g., `getattr(model, 'model_name', repr(model))`) so `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` keying and `test_capability_check_cached` are unambiguously implementable.
findings.finding: The construction-time capability check is described as cached via a module-level set `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` keyed by 'the resolved model string', but for the anthropic path the input is a model OBJECT (not a string). The plan does not specify how to derive the cache key from the object (e.g., `str(model)`, `model.model_name`, `repr(model)`), leaving `test_capability_check_cached` unimplementable without a design decision.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check / CACHE bullet; AC `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the CACHE bullet: 'Key = `model.model_name` (the string identifier pydantic-ai exposes on all model objects); fall back to `repr(model)` if the attribute is absent.' This aligns with the `model.profile` introspection pattern already in the plan.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact cache key value (e.g. `resolved`, `_name`, or `model.model_name`) and where it is extracted, so the `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` set is unambiguously keyed and the cached test can assert the correct key.
findings.finding: The `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` cache key is specified as 'the resolved model string' but this protocol is unresolvable on the Anthropic path: the model OBJECT built by `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` has no inherent string identity attribute referenced in the plan, and `resolved` (the full qualified string like `anthropic:claude-...`) is a different value from `_name` (the stripped bare name passed to the builder). The plan does not specify which string is used as the cache key, how it is derived from the model object, or whether it is `resolved`, `_name`, or something else.
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check', CACHE paragraph; AC line 'test_capability_check_cached'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the construction-time `profile.supports_tools` check is added inside or alongside the existing `_pai_check_config` seam (or inside `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` itself), rather than as a new, unnamed parallel function, to keep all 'LLMConfigError for static misconfiguration' logic co-located.
findings.finding: The plan introduces the capability check as a new, unnamed function in `runner.py` without specifying whether it extends the existing `_pai_check_config` seam (lines 625–637), which is already the designated place for 'refuse config this runner does not honour.' Adding a second construction-time validation path for the same runner creates a parallel configuration-check seam where one already exists.
findings.location: Files section: 'src/rebar/llm/runner.py (the cached construction-time capability check)'
findings.suggested_fix: Place the `profile.supports_tools` guard at the end of `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` (immediately after building the `AnthropicModel` object, where the model object is in scope) and document it alongside the existing `max_retries != 0` guard already there.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks`) to verify that a zero-usage run producing an EMPTY result does NOT emit the warning, covering the suppression branch described in the What section.
findings.finding: The described deliverable 'non-empty result' guard for the usage-plausibility warning has no covering acceptance criterion. The What section specifies 'while the run produced a non-empty result' as an explicit conditional branch of the implementation, but no AC verifies that the warning is suppressed when the result IS empty (the 'benign' case the plan mentions).
findings.location: What section — 'Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING' vs Acceptance Criteria section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the usage-plausibility AC with the body: update `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks` to explicitly state the trigger requires BOTH zero tokens AND a non-empty run result, matching the 'while the run produced a non-empty result' condition in the What section.
findings.finding: The 'What' section specifies the usage-plausibility warning fires only when BOTH zero-token usage AND a non-empty result are present ('while the run produced a non-empty result'), but the AC (`test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks`) omits the non-empty-result condition and only says 'all-zero-usage successful run'. A test written purely from the AC could pass even if the implementation warns on zero-usage empty results — a genuine cross-section contradiction about the trigger condition.
findings.location: What section — 'Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING' paragraph vs Acceptance Criteria — `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC bullet to: 'Usage-plausibility emits a WARNING (asserted via caplog) when a successful run returns a non-empty result AND both input_tokens and output_tokens are 0; does NOT warn on a zero-usage run that produced an empty result; NEVER blocks (`test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pytest fixture (e.g., `autouse` or explicit) in `test_agent_build_invariants.py` that clears `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` before each test that exercises the cache, ensuring `test_capability_check_cached` is not order-dependent.
findings.finding: The module-level `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` set is a process-level mutable singleton; the plan does not specify a test fixture or teardown to reset it between test runs, making `test_capability_check_cached` (the spy-based once-per-model assertion) fragile — a prior test that populates the cache will cause the spy assertion to silently pass or fail depending on execution order.
findings.location: AC: `test_capability_check_cached`; Files: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` (_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED), `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py`
findings.suggested_fix:
In `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py`, add:
```python
import pytest
from rebar.llm import runner

@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def clear_capability_cache():
    runner._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.clear()
    yield
    runner._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.clear()
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pytest fixture (e.g., `autouse` scoped to the test module or each test) that clears `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` before/after each test in `test_agent_build_invariants.py`, preventing cache state from leaking between test cases.
findings.finding: The module-level `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` set has no test-isolation / reset path. A test that populates the cache (e.g., `test_capability_check_cached`) will leave the model string in the set for the rest of the test session; a later test that expects the check to actually fire (e.g., `test_capability_check_fails_fast` if it uses the same model string) may silently skip the check and pass vacuously.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py`
findings.suggested_fix:
In `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py`, add:
```python
import pytest
from rebar.llm import runner

@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def _reset_capability_cache():
    runner._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.clear()
    yield
    runner._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.clear()
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test-isolation mechanism to the plan (e.g., a pytest fixture that monkeypatches `runner._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` to a fresh `set()` for each test that exercises the capability check).
findings.finding: The plan places the construction-time capability check and the _TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED cache in runner.py, but gives no edit target or mechanism for resetting the module-level set between test runs. The tests test_capability_check_cached and test_capability_check_fails_fast will share the same process-level set, making test ordering a source of false-passes (a model string already in the cache skips the check in a later test that expects it to fire).
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) / Construction-time provider tool-calling check; AC test_capability_check_cached
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's 'Files' section / test design, add: 'Each test that exercises the capability check uses a `monkeypatch` fixture: `monkeypatch.setattr(runner_mod, "_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED", set())`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact placement of the capability check relative to tools assembly and model construction in runner.py: specify whether it fires after the tools list is built (around line 224) or elsewhere, resolving the 'tools non-empty' condition's data-dependency.
findings.finding: The capability check's placement is structurally ambiguous relative to the tool-list assembly. The plan gates the check on 'tools non-empty', but in runner.py the tools list is assembled (lines 218–223) BEFORE `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` is called (line 251), while the plan says the check happens 'where rebar constructs an explicit model OBJECT' (i.e., at or inside the Anthropic model construction). This creates a pink-elephant ordering problem: if the check fires inside/adjacent to `_build_retrying_anthropic_model`, the tools list is not yet visible there; if it fires after tools are assembled, it is post-construction not construction-time. The plan does not resolve this ordering.
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; Files section — 'src/rebar/llm/runner.py (the cached construction-time capability check)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'The check fires immediately after the model OBJECT is assigned (after line 253 in the current runner.py), where both `model` and `tools` are in scope; it reads tools from the local list already assembled above.'

findings.finding: The `test_usage_limits_on_run_not_constructor` AC specifies a spy that asserts 'the `Agent(...)` constructor kwargs do NOT include `usage_limits`' but never specifies the interception mechanism. In `runner.py`, `Agent` is imported locally inside `run()` via `_import_pydantic_ai()`, making it non-trivially monkeypatchable — the plan doesn't specify whether to patch `pydantic_ai.Agent.__init__`, `runner._import_pydantic_ai`, or another seam. This is a missing processing-protocol specification co-located with the AC.
findings.location: AC line `test_usage_limits_on_run_not_constructor`; runner.py line 174 (`Agent = _import_pydantic_ai()`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'Use `monkeypatch.setattr(runner_mod, "_import_pydantic_ai", lambda: MockAgent)` where `MockAgent` wraps a real `FunctionModel`-backed Agent and records constructor and `run_sync` kwargs — the same spy pattern already used in `test_cache_model_settings_attached_only_for_anthropic`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or justify the `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` process-level cache: cite a concrete profiling result or scale estimate showing the `hasattr`/bool check is a hot-path bottleneck, or inline the check directly into `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` where the model object is already in hand.
findings.finding: The `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` module-level set is a premature optimization with no supporting evidence. The plan's sole justification is 'a hot loop pays nothing,' but runner.py shows each `run()` call is a multi-second LLM call; the proposed cache guards a single `hasattr`/bool check that adds no measurable overhead at this scale. The plan cites no profiling result, call-rate measurement, or scale estimate as required by G-9.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check: 'CACHE: a module-level set() _TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED'
findings.suggested_fix: Inline the `profile.supports_tools` check directly in `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` (where `model` is already the built `AnthropicModel` object) rather than a module-level cache. The check fires once per `run()` on the anthropic path — the same frequency as the existing `max_retries != 0` guard already in that function.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Instead of a new `test_expected_tools_offered` test, confirm the existing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` (and the `filesystem_tools` unpack in `test_filesystem_tools_are_repo_confined`) already covers the `__name__`-set invariant, and either extend those tests or document why a separate new test adds coverage they do not.
findings.finding: The proposed `test_expected_tools_offered` test in the new file `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py` rebuilds existing coverage. `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py:274` (`test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege`) already asserts `[t.__name__ for t in read_only] == ['show_ticket']` and `{t.__name__ for t in full} == {'show_ticket', 'comment_ticket'}` — which is exactly the `__name__`-set assertion the plan describes. A second test file doing the same check for the same builders is NIH duplication, not additive coverage.
findings.location: AC line: 'Test: `pai_tools.filesystem_tools`/`rebar_tools` offer exactly the expected tool names — fails if a tool is stripped (`test_expected_tools_offered`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit `__name__`-set assertion for `filesystem_tools` to the existing `test_filesystem_tools_are_repo_confined` test (e.g., `assert [t.__name__ for t in tools] == ['read_file', 'list_directory', 'search_files']`) rather than creating a parallel new test file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `test_runner_computes_derived_usage_limits` already covers the `usage_limits`-on-`run_sync` placement invariant; if so, extend that test with an explicit `Agent.__init__` kwargs assertion rather than writing a parallel new test.
findings.finding: The proposed `test_usage_limits_on_run_not_constructor` test rebuilds coverage that already exists. `tests/unit/test_llm_failure_matrix.py:285` (`test_runner_computes_derived_usage_limits`) already spies on `UsageLimits` construction and asserts the runner passes derived `request_limit`/`tool_calls_limit` kwargs. The runner source at lines 328–331 and 344/349/506/525 already demonstrates `usage_limits` is passed to `run_sync`, not the `Agent(...)` constructor. The plan's new test would duplicate spy-on-construction logic without adding a new failure scenario.
findings.location: AC line: 'Test: `run_sync` is called with `usage_limits=` and the `Agent(...)` constructor is NOT — fails on the #1987 mis-placement (`test_usage_limits_on_run_not_constructor`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and reconcile the overlap with the existing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` test in `test_pydantic_ai_runner.py`; either extend it in-place (no new file needed for this AC) or explicitly note that the new file supersedes/consolidates it.
findings.finding: The plan's AC test_expected_tools_offered proposes asserting __name__s on filesystem_tools and rebar_tools in a new file, but tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py already contains test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege (line 274) that asserts exactly {show_ticket, comment_ticket} and [show_ticket] __name__s for rebar_tools, and test_filesystem_tools_are_repo_confined (line 222) exercises filesystem_tools callables. The plan does not acknowledge this overlap, creating a risk of duplicate or inconsistent coverage.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / test_expected_tools_offered; tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py:274
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's 'Tests — tools actually offered' section, note: 'Extends or supersedes test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege in test_pydantic_ai_runner.py; that test should be removed or marked as the canonical location.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the overlap with `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` in `test_pydantic_ai_runner.py` and either extend that existing test to cover `filesystem_tools` names (consolidation) or explicitly document why a separate test file is preferred (e.g. to isolate the build-invariant contract from the tool-behavior tests).
findings.finding: The AC `test_expected_tools_offered` is substantially pre-covered by the existing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` in `test_pydantic_ai_runner.py` (line 274–278), which already asserts `[t.__name__ for t in read_only] == ['show_ticket']` and `{t.__name__ for t in full} == {'show_ticket', 'comment_ticket'}`. The plan's new test adds `filesystem_tools` name-checking but does not acknowledge this overlap or explain what the new test adds over the existing one.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `test_expected_tools_offered`; Files: `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py`

findings.finding: The `model.profile.supports_tools` capability check has a mechanism-correctness gap: on non-Anthropic providers (OpenAI, Gemini), `runner.py` passes the model as a plain string (e.g. `'openai:gpt-4o'`) to `Agent(model_string, ...)` — no model object is instantiated in runner scope at the point the plan describes the check. Strings have no `.profile` attribute, so `model.profile.supports_tools` would raise `AttributeError` on these paths. The plan does not specify how to obtain a pydantic-ai model object for string-based providers at check time.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; runner.py lines 224-225 (`resolved = _pai_model(cfg)` / `model = self._model_override or resolved`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step: after `resolved = _pai_model(cfg)`, when `self._model_override is None` and `tools` is non-empty, call `pydantic_ai.models.infer_model(resolved)` (or equivalent) to obtain a model object and then read `.profile.supports_tools`. Cache by `resolved` string as already planned. This avoids the string-has-no-profile issue. Alternatively, scope the check to providers where a model object is already in hand (the Anthropic path), and note this limitation explicitly.

findings.finding: The construction-time `model.profile.supports_tools` check has no specified protocol for non-Anthropic providers where `model` remains a plain string. In `runner.py`, for OpenAI/Gemini the model variable at the check point is the string from `_pai_model(cfg)` (e.g. `'openai:gpt-4o'`); only Anthropic builds a real model object via `_build_retrying_anthropic_model`. Calling `.profile.supports_tools` on a string yields `AttributeError`. The plan does not specify how to obtain a model object for non-Anthropic providers, nor does it specify an AttributeError fallback rule.
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; runner.py lines 224-225 (model assignment)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'To access `.profile`, resolve the model string to a pydantic-ai model object via `pydantic_ai.models.infer_model(resolved)` before the check. If `AttributeError` is raised (custom adapter), log a WARNING and skip the check (fail-open) — the cache entry is still written so the skip is also only-once.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (or address) the TOCTOU race on `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED`: either note that double-execution is acceptable (and update the 'ONCE' wording to 'at-most-a-few-times'), or use an atomic guard (e.g., `threading.Lock` or a `frozenset`-swap) if strict once-per-process semantics are required.
findings.finding: The check-then-act pattern on `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` (`if model_string not in set: ... set.add(...)`) is a TOCTOU race under concurrent callers: two threads could simultaneously find the model absent, both execute the capability check, and both attempt to add — the check is harmlessly doubled, but if pydantic-ai's `.profile` access has side effects or the intent is strictly-once semantics, this is unspecified.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check (`runner.py`)

findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is asserted as a 'VERIFIED' pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal, but it cannot be confirmed from any observable source in the repo (zero codebase hits) and the plan does not address the mechanistic gap: `_pai_model(cfg)` in runner.py returns a *string*, not a model object — reading `.profile.supports_tools` requires an instantiated model object that does not exist at the point the plan describes the check.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; Files: src/rebar/llm/runner.py
findings.suggested_fix: If pydantic-ai exposes `infer_model(resolved_string)` → model_object offline, document that call. Otherwise, read `model.profile.supports_tools` from the already-constructed `AnthropicModel` returned by `_build_retrying_anthropic_model`, after construction but before `run_sync` — and skip the check entirely on non-Anthropic paths where model-object construction is deferred.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
